step.title: Step 22
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 82, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 31 of about 50 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Good, back to the starting position. Now let me think about this more carefully.
In Level 1:
- The goal was to move the dark gray character to the yellow L-shape's position
- LEFT moved the dark gray character left (toward the yellow L-shape)
- The solution was LEFT 16
In Level 2:
- The dark gray character is at [18,15]-[29,29]
- The yellow L-shape is at [42,3]-[53,17]
- LEFT moves the dark gray character left by 6 cols per action
- RIGHT moves the dark gray character right by 6 cols per action
- UP and DOWN don't work
The yellow L-shape is at rows 42-53, but the dark gray character is at rows 18-29. I can't move vertically, so I can't reach the yellow L-shape's row range.
Wait, maybe the goal in Level 2 is different. Let me think about what could be the goal:
1. Move dark gray to overlap with black character (tried, didn't complete level)
2. Move dark gray to match yellow L-shape's column range (but can't match row range)
3. Something else entirely
Let me look at the board more carefully. The yellow L-shape is at the bottom left, and the dark gray character is at the top left. The black character is at the top right. The divider is in the middle.
What if the goal is to move the dark gray character to the left edge of the board, or to a specific column position? Let me try LEFT 2 to move the dark gray character to cols 3-17, which matches the yellow L-shape's column range.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the Level 1 solution. In Level 1, the dark gray character was moved to the yellow L-shape's position by pressing LEFT 16 times. The dark gray character's final position matched the yellow L-shape's position.
In Level 2, maybe the goal is similar, but the mechanics are different. Since I can only move horizontally, maybe the goal is to have the dark gray character's column range match the yellow L-shape's column range, regardless of the row position.
The dark gray character is at cols 15-29. The yellow L-shape is at cols 3-17. To match the yellow L-shape's column range, I need to move the dark gray character left by 15-3=12 cols. That's 12/6 = 2 LEFT actions.
Let me try LEFT 2 and see if the level completes.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. In Level 1, the dark gray character moved to the exact position of the yellow L-shape, not just matching columns. But in Level 2, I can't move vertically. So maybe the goal is different.
Let me try a different hypothesis: maybe the goal is to move the dark gray character to the left until it reaches the left edge of the board, or until it overlaps with the yellow L-shape's column range.
Let me try LEFT 2 first and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Back to start. Let me try LEFT 2 to align dark gray columns with yellow L-shape columns [3,17].

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try LEFT 2 to align dark gray columns with yellow L-shape
action(['LEFT']*2)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After LEFT 2:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 10 and n['color'] in ['c', 'B', 'Y']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=[{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]-[{max(rows)},{max(cols)}]")
print("Level completed?", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
